draft.introduction: Damascius (c. 462–538 CE), the last head of the Athenian Academy, opens his Problems and Solutions Concerning First Principles by questioning whether the Ineffable can be spoken of at all. His radical apophaticism pushes Neoplatonic negative theology to its absolute limit.

translation.text: Is the One the first principle of all things, or is something beyond the One the first principle? Or is it rather the case that neither “one” nor “beyond” nor “principle” applies to the utterly unknowable? For we call it “ineffable” not because of our inability to express it, but because it is truly beyond all expression and all knowledge and all contact of thought.
translation.original_text: πότερον τὸ ἓν ἐστὶν ἀρχὴ τῶν πάντων

translator_notes.note: Damascius goes further than any previous Neoplatonist: even calling the first principle “One” or “Ineffable” says too much. This hyper-apophaticism represents the logical terminus of the negative theological tradition that began with Plato’s Parmenides.
